profe pulled me aside after class and said 'the test is in 10 days. saturday. pablo too.' I just nodded. didn't trust my voice. he handed me my mouthguard which I'd left on the bench, said 'don't lose this on saturday' and walked off. he's funny. funny is not the word people use for him. funny he is. putting this down, going to read for half an hour, then bed. worth recording. that's enough for tonight. no other notes tonight. logging it. moving on. writing it down so I do not forget. end of thought. ok closing the laptop now.

ok so today was my birthday. june 4. I'm 33 now. I am writing this from the kitchen at 11pm with a glass of mezcal in front of me, very tired, kind of happy. the dog has settled at my feet and is gently snoring, which is the sound of contentment in this household. camila is in the bedroom reading. the apartment smells like the cauliflower-and-charred-lemon thing she made for dinner.

camila planned a quiet dinner — she knew I didn't want a big thing. she invited pablo and his girlfriend marisol, and aldo and his wife. that's it. five people including me. no surprise, no party hat, no instagram round-up. I asked for that explicitly two weeks ago and she remembered to the exact word. she even told her own work friends 'don't post anything' which is the kind of pre-emptive consideration that I notice and don't always thank her for properly. the texture of being well-loved by someone who pays attention is that they remove obstacles from your environment without being asked. she had removed every potential 'someone posts a story tagging seb on his birthday' source by saturday. my birthday on social media was approximately invisible. that is the gift.

she cooked — pablo brought wine — and the menu was: 1) a roasted-cauliflower thing with charred lemon and crispy chickpeas that she pulled from a nytimes recipe but heavily modified, replacing the salt-pickled lemon with a charred lemon-confit she made earlier in the week, 2) a vegetarian birria adapted from her abuela's recipe (she is not actually vegetarian but she did this for me, with mushrooms instead of beef and a deeper guajillo broth than the standard version, with a little smoked tomato to compensate for the meat fat), 3) homemade tortillas, hand-pressed, the kind that make you stop talking when you bite into them, 4) a tres-leches cake from pasteleria ideal in the centro because she knows I have feelings about that specific cake. she put one candle on it. one. I asked why and she said 'because the rest is clutter'. she's so much more put-together than me. I asked if she also believes that wishes work better with one candle and she said 'I don't believe in wishes, I believe in cake' which is, for the record, also why I love her.

pablo gave me a present that was a box of new mouthguards because he said mine smells like 'death and powerade' which is rude but accurate. I have been chewing the same mouthguard for 18 months and pablo is correct that it has reached an aroma not entirely produced by powerade alone. he then gave me a real present, which was a small box containing a white piece of paper with a handwritten note that said 'I will corner you at any belt test you ever take, free, for life'. there was nothing else in the box. I read it and then read it again. it is one of the best gifts I have ever received. aldo gave me a vinyl — a beny moré reissue — that is going on the top shelf when I get to the record cabinet tomorrow. camila gave me a book (the new ann patchett, I'd mentioned it three weeks ago and forgotten I'd mentioned it, she has a memory for these things) and a card. the card said something private that I'm not going to tell you but it made me cry a little. pablo made fun of me for crying and the dog jumped up to see what was happening and aldo's wife laughed and it became one of those moments that's somehow also a memory before it's even ended. you could see it being a memory in real time. that's a strange thing to feel — to know in the present that you're going to remember the present. like a déjà vu in reverse. the technical term for it might be 'meta-memory'. I doubt I'll remember the technical term but I will remember the feeling.

things I'm noticing about being 33: the year is mathematically half-over-but-still-half-to-go feeling, more than 30 had. 30 was a milestone — 33 is just a number, but it's the number my dad was when he had me, so that's looping in my head all night. when my dad was 33 he had a 1-year-old (mateo) and a fledgling teaching career and was already ulcerating from stress. and here I am, no kid, eating cauliflower, working on a side project I might never publish, learning portuguese for a family that's mine by love not law. different shape entirely. I don't know if mine is better but it is mine. and mine is the only one I get to live, so. acceptance is the thing.

marisol asked me at one point what I want for my year of being 33, and I said something glib about 'sleep' and pablo laughed but I've been thinking about her question since. what do I actually want for this year? I want to ship rust to prod. I want to pass the brown belt test next week. I want to figure out the kid thing with camila in a way that doesn't break us. I want to either start the masters application or stop pretending I'm going to. I want to be present for camila's pritzker thing if it happens — and I'm hearing whispers it might. I want to call my mom more. I want to fix my sleep before it breaks. I want to make hugo cry at his 70th — in a good way, with something I made. I want to have, by 34, told camila about MIT. that's a lot for a year. but the candle was already burning so. I made the wishes.

there's a moment from earlier I want to write down because I'll forget it otherwise. when camila brought out the cake, before she lit the candle, she said 'thirty-three, the year of carpenter christ' which is a joke she has been making for two months and which she was clearly delighted to get to deploy properly tonight. catholic-school joke. dad would have rolled his eyes. she was so pleased with herself. that's the camila I want at 38 and 48 and 78 — pleased with herself for landing a stupid joke perfectly timed. that's the laughter I wrote into homework item 1.

pablo and I went out on the fire escape for ten minutes after dinner. I don't smoke and he doesn't smoke but the fire escape is where you go in this apartment when you want to talk without performing for anyone else. he asked me how I was, real version. I said something honest about being scared and tired. he said 'ok' and we were quiet for a minute and he said 'your manager is going to leave'. I said 'how do you know'. he said 'I just know'. he is sometimes right about things he has no business being right about. then he said 'you'll be fine'. I said 'how do you know'. he said 'I just know' again. that's the entire conversation. I went back inside. I kept thinking about it.

camila just came in to ask if I'm coming to bed. saying yes. mezcal half-finished, I'll dump it. dog is already asleep on the bed which is technically against the rules. I'll allow it tonight. one candle, one wish. the wish I made was for camila to laugh at one of my jokes in 25 years and groan and laugh at it. that's all. just that. the rest is clutter.

I notice I have been reflecting more this year than in any previous year of my life, and I think that is because I am 32 and on the cusp of 33, and 33 is one of those numbers that lands. 30 didn't land — 30 felt like an extension of 29. 33 feels like a verdict. it's the age my dad died. that's part of it but not all of it. some of it is the migration, some of it is muñoz, some of it is the prospect of having a kid in two years. some of it is realizing how much of the last decade I sleepwalked through.

the value of writing this out, I think, is that I get to see my own assumptions on the page and notice when they're wrong. there are things I would never say out loud because I'd flinch at hearing them, but I'll type them in a chat at midnight because the chat doesn't flinch back. that's the deal I've made with this medium. it's a kind of half-confession to no one.

I'm going to come back to this in six months and either nod or wince, and I want both versions of me to know I tried to be honest. that is the goal of journaling for me right now. not to predict, not to plan. just to leave an honest record so that future-me cannot lie about who past-me was. that is the bargain.

10 days to brown belt test. let me lay out the prep so I can hold myself accountable. you are going to be my coach for the next 10 days even though you have no idea what a brown belt test feels like. tough. I am going to externalize the plan because plans externalized live, plans internalized rot.

the test at atlas box is roughly: 6 belt grades tested in a single saturday session, but the brown belt one is the longest at about 90 minutes. the structure as profe described it last year when I watched marco do his:

WARMUP (10 min): jumping rope, shadowboxing, dynamic stretches. graded loosely on form but you don't fail here unless you're visibly out of shape or mentally not present. profe watches the warmup more carefully than people think — the warmup tells him whether you're ready in your head, not just your body. if you skip rope like you're somewhere else, you're somewhere else, and the rest of the test will go badly. focus is announced in the warmup.

TECHNIQUE BLOCK (25 min): profe calls out combinations and you execute on the pads. 1-2-low-kick. 1-2-3-2-knee. catch-and-counter sequences. teep-cross-hook-low-kick. you're being graded on stance recovery, hip rotation, and not telegraphing. he likes to throw curveballs — call a combination you haven't drilled in months and watch your hesitation. the brown belt expectation is: zero hesitation. you do the combo or you ask once for a clarification and then you do the combo. no fumbling. when in doubt about a combo, the recovery move is a clean teep — keeps you safe, looks intentional. profe will never say 'good teep' but he will not call it a recovery either. teeps are diplomatic. they say 'I am here, I am thinking, I am not running'.

DEFENSE BLOCK (15 min): partner throws known strikes, you slip/parry/check. you have to read the strike on time. profe is watching for late checks especially — he has said to me approximately 400 times that 'a late check is a bruise'. brown belt means you don't get bruised by predicted strikes. specifically: low-kick checks at the right shin angle (foot turned in, not pointing forward), straight-cross slips (drop the head 4 inches, not 8), hook parries (forearm to forearm, not glove to glove). hook parries with the glove are how you get knocked out — the glove deflects but the fist still arrives. the forearm at the right angle uses your bone against their fist. orthopedically aggressive, defensively necessary.

CLINCH BLOCK (15 min): muay thai clinch — neck-tie, knees, off-balancing. brown belt level: you should be able to win a clinch exchange against a fresh, unfatigued partner. this is one of my stronger blocks because pablo and I drill clinch a lot in the garage and my frame is good. small-frame leverage works against bigger bodies in clinch — you can push their head down and force them off-balance even at a weight disadvantage. the trick I learned from pablo (who learned it from a thai instructor in chiang mai before he was even a blue belt at atlas): clinch is grip strength plus knee timing plus hip awareness. not muscular strength. anyone strong enough to deadlift their own bodyweight has enough strength for clinch. the rest is technique.

SPARRING (20 min): 4 rounds of 3 minutes against rotating partners — usually a brown belt and two blacks pulled in to put you under pressure. you do not have to win. you have to compose. profe explicitly says he fails people who panic. he passes people who get hit clean and stay calm. this is the actual test. everything before is form. this is whether you can do the form when someone is trying to hit you. the worst thing you can do in sparring is start fighting wild — once you go wild, profe sees you've stopped thinking, and he can't grade thinking that's not happening. wild is the failure state. composed is the pass state. and composed under pressure is the difference between brown and purple — purple was about composed in form; brown is about composed in sparring; black, eventually, will be about composed in fatigue.

Q&A (5 min): profe asks you something philosophical or technical. last time it was 'what's the difference between a teep and a push kick' — both are technically correct in english but in profe's mouth one means 'tactical retreat tool' and the other means 'distance control'. nuance test. there's no 'right' answer, just an answer that shows you've been thinking, not just sweating. he asked marco last year 'what's the test for' and marco said 'to show I'm ready' and profe said 'no — to show me you've been training right' and that's the kind of left-curve answer profe rewards. the answer is not the answer he's looking for. the demonstration of having genuinely thought about the question is.

so for the next 10 days, my plan in order:

mon/wed/fri: regular class + 30 extra min on combinations after class. focus the extra time on 1-2-3-2-knee because profe likes that one and I've been getting lazy on the second cross. also: shadow-box the catch-and-counter sequences for 5 minutes before bed each night, just rehearsing the patterns even without contact.

tuesday and thursday evening: pad work with pablo at his place — he's testing too so we're mutual. 90 min each session. drill the catch-and-counter sequences plus clinch. one full sparring round at the end of each, light contact, headgear on. agreement: we are honest with each other about what we see. if I'm dropping my hand on the cross, pablo says 'hand'. one syllable. no pep talk. correction by minimal vocabulary.

saturday before the test (next saturday, day 4 of countdown): no kickboxing. long run (5k) to clear sleep and burn anxiety, NOT a hard session. in the evening, watch one of marco's old recordings of his brown test — pablo has it on his phone — and visualize. visualization sounds woo but I have run it before fights and it works for me.

sunday: yoga (resentful but fine). camila will make me. she's right. mobility is going to matter on the day. I have been doing yoga 1x a month for the last year and her diagnosis is correct that I need 3x a week minimum if I want to fight at 50. she is right. 50 is far. brown belt is in ten days. one project at a time.

day-of (saturday in 10 days): sleep early friday night. wake naturally. big oatmeal-banana breakfast 3 hours before. one mate (just one — caffeine spike too high otherwise). water. nothing new I haven't already eaten before. drive to atlas, arrive 30 min early, slow warmup on my own before profe's group warmup starts. I have been rehearsing the morning routine for two weeks already, the way an actor rehearses a play before opening night. predictable mornings make for clear afternoons. the morning of a fight is not the time to improvise.

worries:
- back is mostly fine but if I tweak it sparring this week I'm cooked. taking it lighter on tuesday than I want to. I keep wanting to push to feel ready and that's the opposite of what readiness looks like at this stage. tapering is the discipline of trusting the work you've already done. it is the hardest discipline in any sport. I am not naturally good at it. I am practicing.
- the q&a always trips me up because profe asks a question and waits in silence and I fill the silence with garbage. lesson: when he asks something, count to 3 before answering. I have written this on the back of my hand metaphorically. I have written it on three index cards literally. one in my gym bag. one on my mirror. one in the visor of la torta.
- I want to look good for camila and my parents and that's the wrong motivation. profe doesn't care about the audience. I shouldn't either. I have to find the thing I want to prove TO MYSELF, not to them. the thing I want to prove to myself is: I can stay composed when someone is trying to hit me. that is the entire test. nothing else.
- new shin guards — I ordered them. they should arrive by wednesday. brown patch will go on the bag after the test, not on the new shin guards (that's superstition I picked up from someone and I'm honoring it).
- the orange-belt-spar in round 1 sometimes gets me hurt because I get cocky and forget the orange belt is allowed to land clean shots. respect the orange. respect everyone in the room. I have been hit harder by orange belts in my life than by black belts, because the orange belt has nothing to lose and goes hard, while the black belt is calibrating and pulling.

ordering this here so I can hold myself to it. you'll get a status update after each gym session probably. sorry in advance. this is going to take over the slack channel of my brain for the next 10 days. I am bracing for the texture of having one big real thing in my brain at all times. it is going to leak into my work, my conversations with camila, the dog walks, the standup meetings. brown belt prep is not a discrete activity for the next 240 hours. it is the lens through which I will see everything else.

put the beny moré reissue on the platter this morning — it's pristine. side A is everything. specifically `dolor y perdón` at 9 minutes in. that's the one. now I'm late to standup. putting this down, going to read for half an hour, then bed. worth recording. that's enough for tonight. no other notes tonight. logging it. moving on. writing it down so I do not forget. end of thought. ok closing the laptop now. noting it, going to bed. enough for now.

found a real edge case in the encoder — geography points near the antimeridian (longitude ±180) get serialized differently by postgis depending on whether they were inserted as a wkt or via ST_GeogFromText with the explicit srid. it doesn't matter for our use case because brújula doesn't deliver to fiji. but I noted it in a TODO comment so future-me knows.

tests are not going to catch it because we don't have antimeridian fixtures. fine for now. someday-maintainer or open-source extraction can deal with it. wrote it up in MIGRATION.md under 'known limitations' so if someone files an issue eventually we have a writeup ready: 'antimeridian roundtrip non-idempotent — postgis-side normalization preference'. keeps the spec honest about its limits which is more important than the limit itself.

the engineering principle here: never let an edge case go undocumented even if it's irrelevant to your service. tomorrow's brújula might deliver to fiji. the code shouldn't have to relearn the constraint. write it down once. inherit it forever. and the broader principle: known limitations are the cheapest documentation a project ever produces — if you list them honestly, future contributors trust the rest of the doc more, because they can see you weren't pretending. honesty is a documentation strategy, not just a moral one.

and the more I look at this the more I think the real bug is upstream of the code — it's that I keep promising 'I'll just look at it for ten minutes' and then it's two hours and Camila is asking why I'm still at the desk. yesterday she came in at 11:40, didn't say anything, just stood in the doorway with Nube and stared at the back of my head until I closed the laptop. she's getting better at not saying it out loud. I'm getting worse at not requiring her to say it out loud. that ratio is going the wrong direction and I see it.

writing this out is helping more than I expected. the thing about typing the problem is that the typing is slower than the thinking, and the slowness forces a sequencing on the thoughts that the brain doesn't want to do on its own. half the time when I rubber-duck like this the bug reveals itself in the act of describing. the other half it's still a real bug but at least I have an artifact I can paste into the team channel tomorrow. either way it beats spinning in my head at 11pm.

thing I keep noticing — and this is sort of meta but worth recording — is that every time I describe these problems out loud I find a hole in my model. the wkb byte order question, for example, I literally don't have a confident answer to right now: is the SRID prefix on EWKB always little-endian regardless of the wkb byte order flag, or does it follow the wkb endianness? I'd assume the latter but I haven't verified. that's the kind of thing where if I assumed wrong, integration tests pass on dev where everything is little-endian, then explode in some weird big-endian context I'll never see. PostGIS docs are my friend tomorrow.

carla is going to come back from leave and I want to be able to walk her through this in 20 minutes flat — not 'here's everything I tried', but 'here's the decision tree, here's where we landed, here's why, here's the test coverage, here's the open risks'. that's the standard. if I can't do that, I haven't actually solved the problem; I've just spent time on it. there's a difference and daniela has hammered it into me over a year of 1:1s.

I'm getting worse at not requiring her to say it out loud. that ratio is going the wrong direction and I see it.

pad work at pablo's tonight. 90 minutes. his garage smells like rubber mat and gasoline and we put on a piazzolla record because he indulges me. we drilled the catch-and-counter sequences for 45 min until they felt automatic and then he called combinations randomly and I had to react. the third round of random calls he started swearing at me in the middle of the combo to try to break my focus and it WORKED for about 20 seconds — I dropped a hook — but then I got my head back. that's the kind of dirty practice profe doesn't have time for in class. that's what pablo is for. brother-tier friend.

after we wrapped, marisol came out with mezcal — pablo allowed himself one, I said no, sticking to my no-mezcal-until-after-the-test rule — and we sat on the steps for 20 minutes. he said 'you're gonna pass'. I said 'you don't know that'. he said 'I know it'. that's pablo. unconditional confidence in friends, scathing about everyone else.

he also said something I want to remember: 'the orange belts at the test are going to come at you hard because they have nothing to lose. don't get cocky. eat the first one cleanly and adjust.' I have been told this before but never quite this way. eat the first one. the first hit is information. once you've taken it, the rest of the round is data-driven. if you spend the first round avoiding contact, you will spend it tense, and tense fighters lose. eat it and breathe.

the piazzolla was libertango again. he plays it whenever we're prepping for something hard. I don't think he knows he does it. it's a tell. the violins on libertango have an urgency that matches the heart rate of someone about to spar. something about that bow attack lines up with the rhythm of clean strike-and-recover. there is no science behind this claim. it is purely a piece of pablo lore. I will continue to believe it.

off mezcal until after the test. mate only. water. boring. it's fine. pablo's been doing it longer and is grumpier about it. I told him 8 days isn't a lifetime. he said 8 days is 8 lifetimes when you can't have a glass of wine. and that's the kind of thing I notice now in a way I didn't five years ago. small inheritances become heavier as you age, not lighter. I keep noticing this pattern. might message my mom about it tonight actually. she'll know. she watched abuela do it for thirty years. seven years and counting. I plan to keep using it until I can't anymore. then it goes to whoever is next.

I PASSED.

I'm writing this from the bathroom of atlas box because I needed five minutes alone and there's confetti that pablo somehow snuck in being thrown at people. nine of us tested. all nine passed today. it's been a quiet group all year — we leveled up together — and something about that fact is hitting me in a way I didn't expect. nine adults, mostly working professionals, who all chose to spend 90 minutes today getting punched at to earn a piece of fabric. and we all earned it. all nine. there is something about the shared completion that I want to mark — when nine people show up at 9am on a saturday, sweat through the same 92 minutes, and walk out together with new belts, the bond is not 'colleagues' or even 'friends'. it is closer to 'survivors of a small mutual ordeal'. that's a relationship category that does not have a clean english name. atlas-box-class-of-month-8.

the test was 92 minutes. I'm wrecked. my left calf is going to bruise from a clinch knee in the third sparring round, my right knuckle is split open on the top from a defensive parry that hit gabriel's elbow, my ribs ache from a body shot I half-blocked. but I PASSED. and so did pablo, and so did marco's little cousin who was testing for orange. orange! sixteen years old, grinning like he won the lottery. he tried to high-five me and I missed his hand and accidentally smacked his shoulder. he laughed. I laughed. that high-five attempt is going to be one of the moments I remember. these belt tests, you remember the misfires more than the form.

I'll write the long version later — for now, the moments that stuck:

1) the technique block: profe called the 1-2-3-2-knee combo and I did it cleanly first time and he didn't say anything, which from profe is full-throated praise. the second time he called it I was on autopilot and missed the second cross entirely. he saw it and his eye twitched. on the third call I locked back in. he didn't comment on the miss. I think the recovery counted more than the miss. that is — and I'm now realizing this — a profe principle: he is grading not the absence of mistakes but the presence of recovery. all fighters miss. brown belts come back from the miss. orange belts dwell on it. the difference between an orange belt and a brown belt is the millisecond between miss and recover.

2) the q&a: he asked me 'what's the difference between fighting tired and fighting smart'. I counted to three (I REMEMBERED to count) and then I said 'fighting tired is when you're running out of body. fighting smart is when you've already been running out of body for two rounds and you're now winning anyway.' he gave a single nod. that was either pass or fail. apparently pass. I have been thinking about the framing he asked all day. 'tired' versus 'smart' was the actual binary he wanted me to decompose. I'm not 100% sure I gave the answer he wanted, but I gave AN answer and didn't fill the silence. that was the win.

3) the sparring: third round, against gabriel — black belt, 6'2", been at atlas for 11 years. he caught me with a body hook in the second minute that took my air. I went into half-clinch, ate the knee that's going to bruise, but I didn't break composure. I tied him up, dragged it to the bell. that round did not feel like winning. profe later told me it was the round he watched closest. 'you stayed,' he said. 'you stayed.' just those two words. the entire test compressed into two words. I am going to put that on a sticky note above my monitor: STAYED. when I am tempted to spiral on a code review or panic on a deploy, the sticky note is going to remind me that the only thing I have to do is stay. stay in the round. stay in the body. stay in the work. composure is not a feeling, it is an action — the action of staying.

4) when he tied the brown belt on me, he leaned in and said in spanish, very quiet, 'next is the long one'. that's all he said. 'la siguiente es la larga.' meaning black. 18 months minimum, usually two-plus years. for some reason I started crying right there. like, on the mat, in front of everyone. pablo whooped from the side. camila wasn't there — she was at a foster + partners thing she could not reschedule, a partner-track conversation that mattered to her career — but I called her from the parking lot and she screamed. like, full vocal scream of joy. people in nearby cars looked. she said 'I'm coming home now, who do I have to call to make this not happen tonight'. I told her not to bail. she said 'I love you, I'm so proud, I'm bailing in 30 min.' she bailed in 30 min.

the small detail I want to record: when he leaned in and said 'la siguiente es la larga', he did not pat my shoulder. he did not smile. he just said it and stepped back to the next person. the gesture was so devoid of theater that it landed harder than any motivational speech could have. profe does not give pep talks because he understands that pep talks are about the speaker. he gives statements of fact about the listener. 'next is the long one' is not motivation. it is information. it is also a vote of confidence delivered in the form of expectation. he expects me to keep showing up for 156 weeks. the expectation IS the confidence.

there is also a thing I felt for the first time in any sport: the certainty that I will keep going. before today, I have been the kind of person who starts things and stops them. duolingo for languages I abandoned. an ml side project that never shipped. running, on and off. mountain biking, abandoned. piano lessons, three months. atlas box has been the first physical practice I have stuck with for more than 18 months and the brown belt is the first physical achievement I have earned past the introductory phase of any pursuit. and what I felt today, on the mat, after profe stepped back, was: I am going to keep going. all the way. not because I want to or because I should but because I have already decided to, and the deciding has the weight of a fact rather than an aspiration. that is a feeling I have not had about anything else in my life. I am holding it carefully. I do not want to lose the texture of it.

brown. belt. brown. I'm going to look at this number for a minute. brown. the bag patch order goes in tonight. mom and dad are flying in for the next test, no matter when it is, they have already declared it. mom has already started talking about flights. pablo says he wants to do mexican muay thai team trials in 2027 and wants me as a corner. I haven't said no. I haven't said yes. I've said 'let me get a beer first'. he's said 'no beer until you have an answer.' he's joking. he's mostly joking. I'll say yes. I'll say yes after I've had three days to enjoy not having said anything. but I'll say yes.

the brown belt is wrapped around my waist as I write this. it is heavier than the purple was. that's not technical. it is heavier emotionally. it knows it is closer to black. it carries that gravity. I am going to take it off in a minute and put it in my bag with respect. I am not going to wash it. I am going to keep tonight's sweat in the cloth as a record of how it was earned. that's a small ritual. I have been collecting small rituals lately. I think 33 might be the year of small rituals.

it's the morning after and I'm at cardinal with mate and a croissant and I want to write the long version of the test while it's still vivid because I know in two weeks it'll be hazy and overwritten by whatever's next. my left calf is the color of an eggplant. my right knuckle is scabbed. but I've slept and showered and I have time before standup.

I woke at 7 saturday after a real sleep — better than I'd expected, the visualization night before helped, plus I had cut caffeine to one mate the previous afternoon. oatmeal banana breakfast, one mate. drove la torta to atlas, got there 8:45 for a 9 start. profe was already there setting up cones for the warmup. he saw me and said only 'qué tal' and went back to the cones. that's profe. on test day he doesn't pep-talk anyone. you walked through the door, that's the pep talk. when I think about the people who have shaped me as an adult, profe is in the top three, even though we have probably exchanged fewer than 200 words in the eighteen months I've trained with him. the words have all landed. that is its own kind of teaching — saying very little but saying it precisely.

the nine of us testing lined up. profe addressed us for maybe 30 seconds total. 'we don't fail you for being tired. we fail you for being scared. don't be scared. be tired.' that's the speech. that's the whole speech. he turned around and started the warmup. I had been preparing myself for some ceremony. the ceremony was: nothing. that was the ceremony. and looking back this morning, the absence of ceremony was the right call. ceremony makes you self-conscious. self-conscious people fail belt tests because they spectate themselves rather than execute. profe took the spectator-frame off the table by refusing to perform.

warmup felt fine. technique block I was nervous in the first 5 minutes — my 1-2-low-kick had no rhythm, profe didn't say anything but I felt his eyes — and then around minute 7 something clicked and I dropped into the rhythm. I think the click was: I stopped trying to do well and started trying to do it cleanly. small reframe, big effect. I have written this down in my notes app under 'general life lessons' which is a pretentious folder I should probably rename. the entry reads: 'do well = vague performance metric measured against unknown observer. do cleanly = concrete process metric measured against your own checklist. always pick the second one.' I am going to apply this to code reviews next week. and to camila conversations. and to my mom phone calls. cleanly is a more useful frame than well, in basically every domain.

defense block I almost ate one early — late check on a low kick from a guy I should have read. bruise on the inside thigh, not bad. profe noted it on his clipboard. after that one I locked in. the rest of the block I read everything coming in time. there is a very specific feeling when defense lock-in happens — the visual processing speeds up. it is not that you are faster. it is that you are seeing earlier. brown belt defense is about seeing what is coming 200ms before it arrives. that 200ms is the difference between a clean check and a bruise.

clinch block was actually my strongest. years of grappling with pablo paid off — I have small-frame leverage, I get the inside neck-tie naturally. won 3 of 4 exchanges. the one I lost was against eduardo who's 30 pounds heavier and just walked me into the wall. fine. clinch against a 30-pound advantage is a problem of physics, not technique. I would lose that exchange ten times out of ten and that is acceptable.

sparring is the part everyone fears. 4 rounds, 3 minutes each, 30 second rest. round one: an orange belt — basically gentle, I controlled distance, lots of teeps to keep him at range. (I remembered pablo's note: don't get cocky on the orange belt. I controlled distance and ate one exploratory body shot from him cleanly to give him the data he was looking for. he relaxed. the round became an exchange of feedback rather than a fight. that is the right way to spar an orange belt at a brown belt test. give them the round they are testing for, take what they have to give, do not show off.) round two: a brown belt, even-level — went well, traded clean, ended in a brief clinch I won. round three: gabriel, the black belt I mentioned. that body hook. that knee. I will remember that round for years. I lost that round on points if anyone was scoring, but I did not break, I did not stop moving, and I did not lose composure. round four: another brown — by now I was running on fumes, technique a little sloppy, but I did not stop and I did not flinch. the round-4 fatigue answer was the answer to the q&a question profe was about to ask. it was almost as if profe had timed his question to land while I had just demonstrated the answer in body language. maybe he had. profe is a careful man.

q&a I covered yesterday. profe asked his question, I counted to 3, I gave the answer about fighting tired and fighting smart, he nodded once. that was the whole interaction. five seconds of speech for ten years of meaning.

then profe walked down the line and tied belts. mine first, alphabetical order I guess. brown belt, brown patch on the bag tonight. when he leaned in about the 'long one' I think — I don't know — I think part of why I cried was I realized: profe is telling me he sees me going further. and he doesn't say things he doesn't mean. he doesn't waste words. if he says 'next is the long one' he's actually saying 'I see you on a black belt path' and that's a thing he had to choose to say versus a thing he could have left unsaid. and the choice to say it carries the weight that the words alone don't carry. I am going to spend the rest of my life with various authority figures and I will remember this moment when I have to communicate confidence to people who report to me. the model is: don't pep-talk. don't motivate. say a true thing about the listener that they can carry forward. and only say it once. repetition would dilute it.

black is two-plus years out. that's so far. and yet — sitting here this morning, with my calf throbbing and a coffee getting cold next to my mate — I kind of believe it. for the first time in any sport, in any pursuit, I kind of believe it. the rust migration I believe in because it's mostly a math problem. masters apps I sometimes believe in but the gatekeeping is real. but black belt — that's just showing up. 156 weeks of showing up. I can do that. I have already done the first 90.

there's also a thing I didn't expect to feel: pride that's NOT about anyone else. usually when I do well at something my first instinct is to call my mom, to text pablo, to put it where someone else can witness it. yesterday I cried before pablo whooped. I cried for me. that's a small thing but a different thing. I have been performing my achievements for an audience for so long that I'd forgotten you could just have one for yourself. and the entry in 'general life lessons' that I want to add this morning: practice unwitnessed achievement. do something well, hold it, do not announce it. let the doing be the reward. one thing a month minimum. it does not have to be impressive. it has to be unannounced. that is the discipline of self-relationship that I have been failing for a decade and that I am going to start practicing now.

okay. cardinal is filling up. people working on laptops glaring at me for using the long table to write. I'll close. brown belt. one more rung to climb. and on the way to standup I will also start drafting the rust migration status doc daniela asked for, because the work doesn't stop and that's also a thing about getting older. you celebrate for an evening. you get back to it the morning after. you do not earn rest by passing tests. you earn the next test by passing this one. fairly, I would have liked one full day off. I will allow myself sunday.

back at work after the test. monday standup, andrés had pushed three small PRs over the weekend (eager, told you) and one of them was actually really good — refactored the request validation into a tower middleware so I don't have to repeat the unwrap-or-bad-request boilerplate in every handler. approved.

also: roberto's name was in the all-hands invite for tomorrow. nobody knows why. nobody asks. I feel a thing in the air. daniela's smile is back to that 'I know something' tightness. I caught her in the hallway after standup and she just said 'tomorrow' and walked off. that's all the confirmation I needed that whatever is coming is real. asking the team to do focus work tomorrow morning before the all-hands; whatever the announcement is, it's going to occupy everyone's attention for the rest of the day.

andrés's pr was technically clean but, more importantly, it was thoughtful — he wrote the middleware in a way that preserves the typed extractor pattern axum does well, instead of doing the easy thing and using `Request<Body>` and string-typing everything. juniors who write thoughtful code want to grow into seniors who write thoughtful code. juniors who write 'just works' code stay in junior territory. andrés is on the right trajectory. I told him so on slack. he sent back a thumbs up that may or may not have been embarrassed. compliments to early-career engineers should be specific and dropped in slack rather than said face-to-face — they need re-readability, the chance to look at the message a few times across the week. spoken praise evaporates. typed praise is referenceable.

and the more I look at this the more I think the real bug is upstream of the code — it's that I keep promising 'I'll just look at it for ten minutes' and then it's two hours and Camila is asking why I'm still at the desk. yesterday she came in at 11:40, didn't say anything, just stood in the doorway with Nube and stared at the back of my head until I closed the laptop. she's getting better at not saying it out loud. I'm getting worse at not requiring her to say it out loud. that ratio is going the wrong direction and I see it.

writing this out is helping more than I expected. the thing about typing the problem is that the typing is slower than the thinking, and the slowness forces a sequencing on the thoughts that the brain doesn't want to do on its own. half the time when I rubber-duck like this the bug reveals itself in the act of describing. the other half it's still a real bug but at least I have an artifact I can paste into the team channel tomorrow. either way it beats spinning in my head at 11pm.

thing I keep noticing — and this is sort of meta but worth recording — is that every time I describe these problems out loud I find a hole in my model. the wkb byte order question, for example, I literally don't have a confident answer to right now: is the SRID prefix on EWKB always little-endian regardless of the wkb byte order flag, or does it follow the wkb endianness? I'd assume the latter but I haven't verified. that's the kind of thing where if I assumed wrong, integration tests pass on dev where everything is little-endian, then explode in some weird big-endian context I'll never see. PostGIS docs are my friend tomorrow.

carla is going to come back from leave and I want to be able to walk her through this in 20 minutes flat — not 'here's everything I tried', but 'here's the decision tree, here's where we landed, here's why, here's the test coverage, here's the open risks'. that's the standard. if I can't do that, I haven't actually solved the problem; I've just spent time on it. there's a difference and daniela has hammered it into me over a year of 1:1s.

the brown patch came in the mail. ironed it onto the bag tonight. camila said I look proud and I said 'I look like a guy with a brown patch' and she said 'same thing'. she took a picture for her parents. hugo replied to the family chat: 'parabéns, faixa marrom'. the dog tried to sniff the iron. drama averted. and that's the kind of thing I notice now in a way I didn't five years ago. small inheritances become heavier as you age, not lighter. I keep noticing this pattern. might message my mom about it tonight actually. she'll know. she watched abuela do it for thirty years. seven years and counting. I plan to keep using it until I can't anymore. then it goes to whoever is next.

okay let me capture the current state of the rust migration so I have something to point at when I write the next status update for daniela. she's asked for a written one this week. you get the brain dump version, she gets the cleaned-up version.

DONE: core domain types (Driver, Vehicle, RouteRequest, RouteResponse, ServiceArea wrapping postgis geography(Polygon,4326)). the postgis encoder, fuzz-tested, integration-tested. the nearest-drivers handler with proper JoinSet. middleware for auth/validation/logging. sqlx pool config. error type sanitized. integration tests against postgis docker in CI.

NOT DONE: the full route-optimization endpoint (this is the hard one — python uses or-tools, we'll port the simpler heuristic). driver-status websocket. batched scoring (waiting on the downstream team for 4 weeks). shadow-traffic comparison harness. prometheus metrics. runbook docs. websocket-specific load tests.

TIMELINE I AM TELLING DANIELA: 4 more weeks for route-opt + websocket (probably 6). 2 more weeks for the comparison harness. 2 weeks for staging soak. then cutover with feature flag. so realistically prod in 12-14 weeks, more probably 16, given my .8x estimate track record and the fact that the new manager will probably want a 'kickoff' meeting that adds 1-2 weeks of overhead.

RISKS: 1) or-tools rust bindings rotting. backup: python sidecar via grpc. 2) shadow-traffic might reveal rust/python output diffs from random seed differences. need a tolerance metric. 3) sqlx pre-release: if upstream changes the api, rework. 4) team capacity: andrés great but junior, carla back in 5 weeks, I'm running this with brown belt and therapy. 5) manager change in 4 weeks. unknown. could be 1-week handoff or 2-week realign sink.

daniela likes status docs that admit risks. anything that says 'on track no issues' she discounts. she has trained me into honest reporting. I will preserve that habit through the next manager. honest reporting is how I want to be measured.

nube did a full hour alone today while we were both at work. doña marta reports zero whining. peanut butter kong is the chemical weapon of choice. doña marta says she's even started napping when we leave instead of sitting at the door. that's the cure. Camila and I are okay. just noting the pattern. these little moments are the thing. I keep telling myself. no other notes tonight. logging it. moving on. writing it down so I do not forget. end of thought. ok closing the laptop now. noting it, going to bed. enough for now.

daniela's leaving brújula.

she pulled me into a 1:1 yesterday and told me before the all-hands. she's joining a series A startup as VP eng — about 25 people, infra-tooling space, founders ex-stripe. she's already accepted. last day in 4 weeks.

she told me before the announcement because she said and I quote 'I'm not going to leave you to find out from a slack message after you carried the rust migration on your back for 8 months'. that's daniela exactly. that line is exactly the shape of how she manages. when somebody shapes you as a professional over the course of two years, you start to recognize the molds they pour you through. the 'don't let people find out from a slack message' mold is one of hers. I will carry it forward. when I have to deliver hard news, I will deliver it in person, in advance, with 1:1 cushioning. that is daniela's contribution to my management style — even though I am not yet a manager.

she's been my manager since I joined brújula in late 2023. she's the one who put me up for senior in month 2 — even with the awkwardness of camila being on bereavement leave in the background, she made the case at promo committee and it landed. the promo bump was 18% in cash plus equity refresh — substantial but more meaningfully a ratification of a level I had not believed I was at. she's the one who quietly told me, last november, that the postgres outage 'wasn't your fault, but it's in your hands now to make it not happen again' — and then trusted me to lead the rust migration even though I'd never tech-led anything bigger than a feature in my entire career. wizeline never gave me that. it was a lateral career, banked on consulting hours and account expansion, no ownership over a thing. before that, kueski was my first job out of school and they used me as a junior who could be moved between teams as needed. daniela handed me ownership of a service. she's the only manager who has.

I didn't know what to say in the 1:1. I think I said 'congratulations' three times, all of which sounded fake to my own ear, the way 'congratulations' always sounds when you mean it but the word has been worn smooth by overuse. she let me sit there. then I asked the only real question: 'who replaces you'. she said it's not announced yet. but I could read her face — she knew, and the face she made was the face of a person who has reservations and is being professional about them. her professional face is famous in our org. it's the face she wears in front of the cto. it gives nothing away. she wore it with me for ten seconds and then dropped it and said 'I think you'll be okay. you've gotten very good at this. but it'll be different.'

I am — this is going to sound dramatic — I am genuinely scared. because daniela operated as a buffer between the engineering team and the political layer at brújula. the cto, the cpo, the founders. she absorbed the BS and let us cook. her successor may or may not do that, and from what I've seen of how the cpo describes engineering work in all-hands ('story points' he says, even though we've never used story points, because that's what he learned at a previous job), the political layer is going to want to push their preferred frameworks down onto us the moment the buffer is gone. and I have like 12-16 weeks left on a project that requires a manager who SHIELDS, not one who AMPLIFIES the political layer. losing the shield in the middle of the project is the worst possible timing. she is going at the end of month 8. shadow traffic is week 12, soak is week 14, cutover is week 16. that means I will be cutting over to prod under a manager I have never worked with, on a project they did not authorize, with their first 6-8 weeks of management overhead bleeding into my critical path. the math is bad. there is no version of this math that is good. I have to find the version that is least bad.

plus: rec letters. if I want to apply to anything in the future — and I have been quietly thinking about MIT for like three weeks now though I haven't said it out loud to anyone, not even camila, not yet — daniela is THE rec letter. she's already said yes when I floated it casually two months ago, in a 1:1 conversation that I framed as 'just thinking about it'. now I'm wondering if I should formalize that NOW, before she's a former-manager floating away. she'll forget the granular wins if I wait. people forget specifics. I want her writing a letter that mentions the postgres post-mortem by name, the rust encoder by detail, the way I handled andrés's onboarding — and she will only do that if she's still in the headspace where those things are present. once she's at the new place dealing with new fires, my fires will fade. her brain will be full of new context. my context will get compressed into a paragraph she dictates from memory two months later. compression loses nuance. nuance is the whole point of a strong rec letter. I need to ask now. tomorrow morning.

the deeper layer: daniela has been one of three people in my professional life who has actually changed me. profe at atlas box is one. pablo over twenty years of friendship is the third. the rest of the people I have worked with have polished or scratched me. these three have actually shaped. and I am about to lose direct access to one of the three. I will keep her as a former-manager mentor, of course. she will still be a phone call away. but the daily-formative pressure of working under her stops. the culture in my reports that came from her culture in my reports, that ends in 4 weeks. I have to absorb the residue and pay it forward, because there will be no continuing supply.

ordering, immediately:

1) tell camila tonight. she will have feelings about this — daniela has been a stabilizing force in our domestic life too, indirectly, by being the reason I haven't melted down at work for 18 months. camila has met her once at a brújula event and described her as 'the only adult in the company'.

2) tomorrow morning: send daniela a calm 'thanks for telling me first, can we get a coffee in your last week, also one ask I want to make' message. don't ask for the rec letter in the email. ask in person.

3) start thinking about who replaces her and what that means for routeopt. given the politics, I'd guess a hire from outside — someone with a 'managing managers' resume, dropped in to professionalize the engineering org. that would be bad for routeopt.

4) draft a one-page rust-migration status doc that's simple enough for a non-rust-fluent manager to follow. explain WHY the encoder problem was hard, in english, without code. the new manager is going to inherit a project they don't understand and I don't want them to flatten it in their first week to look decisive.

5) tell pablo. he won't gossip. he will say something blunt that I need to hear.

more tomorrow. I'm tired. need to tell camila. dog is unaware. dog still wants dinner. life persists. life persists in a way that is, frankly, helpful — the dog needs dinner regardless of who runs my engineering team. there is something steadying about a hungry dog when your professional sky is shifting. domestic gravity is real and useful.

I notice I have been reflecting more this year than in any previous year of my life, and I think that is because I am 32 and on the cusp of 33, and 33 is one of those numbers that lands. 30 didn't land — 30 felt like an extension of 29. 33 feels like a verdict. it's the age my dad died. that's part of it but not all of it. some of it is the migration, some of it is muñoz, some of it is the prospect of having a kid in two years. some of it is realizing how much of the last decade I sleepwalked through.

the value of writing this out, I think, is that I get to see my own assumptions on the page and notice when they're wrong. there are things I would never say out loud because I'd flinch at hearing them, but I'll type them in a chat at midnight because the chat doesn't flinch back. that's the deal I've made with this medium. it's a kind of half-confession to no one.

I'm going to come back to this in six months and either nod or wince, and I want both versions of me to know I tried to be honest. that is the goal of journaling for me right now. not to predict, not to plan. just to leave an honest record so that future-me cannot lie about who past-me was. that is the bargain.

more on daniela because I haven't slept well on this and writing things out helps. it is 1am and the dog has been twice to check on me and I am drinking the third mate of the night, which is a violation of my own rules, but rules are for people whose managers aren't leaving.

daniela hired me in late 2023, when I was at the end of my wizeline tenure. she found me on linkedin via a mutual connection. did three interviews herself, including a system design that was the most generous one I've ever had — 'design a real-time driver-tracking system. assume 5000 drivers, 50 cities, 30 second update cadence.' that was it. she let me ask questions for 20 minutes before drawing anything. at the end she said 'okay, we have a job offer' and I said 'really, you don't want another round' and she said 'I've heard enough.'

the first six months at brújula she gave me space. I made mistakes — underestimated a refactor in month 3, introduced a regression in month 4 that needed a hotfix at 11pm — and she didn't blink. private feedback after the dust settled, public protection both up and down the chain. the cpo asked her in an all-hands once 'who shipped that regression last week' and she just said 'it was an oversight that's been corrected, and the team has rolled out additional pre-merge guards.' didn't name me.

I learned more in those six months about being a senior than I had in 7 years prior. she modeled it. I copied it. when andrés joined, I gave him a small piece of the encoder to own. when he made a mistake last month, I rolled it back without a public scolding. that's the daniela method. I am one of her copies in the world now. probably a dozen of us scattered across cdmx, walking around managing in her shape.

and I am — I'm a little hurt. that's the embarrassing emotion under the professional concern. I know it's irrational. she SHOULD take a VP role. but a piece of me is whining 'how could you leave WHILE WE'RE STILL BUILDING THE THING'. like a kid whose parent is going on a business trip the week of his birthday. I'm 33. I should know better. and yet.

the MORE useful emotion: anxiety about the new manager. they will come from elsewhere. they will not have spent 18 months learning what to expect from each member of our team. first impressions calcify in management contexts faster than anywhere else — a manager forms 'is sebastián a strong contributor' opinion in the first three weeks. the first three weeks are about to be three of my hardest weeks of the year. this is going to hurt my professional standing during a window when I cannot afford it.

action items: 1) email daniela tomorrow morning about the rec letter. 2) write the one-page status doc. 3) ask daniela in her last week for her honest read of who's replacing her. 4) figure out my own emotional reaction. add to muñoz session. 5) start telling camila about MIT.

okay. typed it out. going to bed. brown belt patch on the bag glows when I look at it. I need to look at things mine and stable while a thing that has felt mine and stable becomes neither.

sent daniela the email. asked for the rec letter. she replied in 17 minutes. yes. of course. with a heart emoji which is wildly out of character for her. that hit. I read the email three times. first thinking about the letter, second thinking about the heart emoji, third just sitting with it.

she also added 'and I'll get the letter to MIT before my last day, not after — better for both of us if it's signed under the brújula domain'. she is thinking about the optical detail of which email domain the letter comes from — institutional letters from a still-active manager carry more weight than from a recently-departed one. that level of attention to outcome is how she has always been. it is also a kindness that takes the form of a logistical decision. she is not going to tell me she cares. she is going to act in ways that demonstrate care, on the level of choosing the right email domain. that is the daniela language. I have been learning to read it for two years.

I showed camila the email at dinner. she read it twice and said 'she is going to write you a great letter'. I said 'how do you know'. she said 'people who reply in 17 minutes with a heart emoji are people who have been thinking about you'. that is also a true thing. people who would write a great letter are people who would also reply in 17 minutes. those are the same set of people. fast warm response is a leading indicator of the quality of follow-through. add this to the list of things I should remember about people: how fast and warm they respond when you ask them for something inconvenient is the most reliable measurement of how much they value the relationship. forget what they say. measure response time and warmth. 17 minutes and a heart emoji is the empirical signal.

the all-hands was today. roberto sánchez has been announced as the new engineering manager for the logistics platform team starting in 4 weeks (when daniela's last day hits). he'll be remote at first from monterrey, then relocating to cdmx in month 9 or 10.

first impression — based on 12 minutes of a town-hall video call where he was mostly listening and 4 minutes where he was talking — let me be fair before I get cynical, because cynicism is cheap. he comes from oxxo's tech org — way bigger than brújula, will know things about scale we don't. he's apparently the one who unwound a disastrous ms365 migration there, so he can untangle things. his english is good. he asked one technically substantive question in the q&a — about our ci/cd pipeline times — which suggests he reads the engineering excellence dashboard. he made eye contact in the camera, which sounds dumb but is a real tell.

LESS fair / concerning things: he used 'visibility into individual contributor output' in his three-minute intro. that's a flag. anyone who frames IC output as requiring 'visibility' is a manager who invents metrics. his linkedin: seven jobs in twelve years. that pattern is either 'gets pushed out' or 'jumps before being pushed' and either way isn't stability. he dropped 'we'll align on cadence and rituals' twice. consultant-speak that means more meetings. he did not ask any question about the products we ship. he asked about pipelines, cadence, visibility. all process. zero substance. zero curiosity about the actual logistics problem. daniela's face during his intro was a mask. for daniela, completely neutral is bad. he closed with 'I'm excited to drive engineering excellence here.' the verb 'drive' applied to people. driving cars is fine. driving people is a tell.

I'm going to give him a fair shake. 30 days minimum before I form a hardened opinion. but my early read is: this is a manager who manages managers, dropped into a team that needs a manager who manages engineering. and if it goes sideways with him — well. MIT is on the table. one rec letter locked. I should start the application in earnest. it's been an idea. it should become a project. tonight I will tell camila. by end of week I want a calendar with milestones: GRE waiver request, personal statement draft 1, transcript order, recommender list. if MIT is real, MIT gets a kanban board.

started porting the greedy + 2-opt heuristic from python to rust today. the python is — I have to be honest — not pretty. it's a 600-line file with three different conventions for representing routes (a list of lists, a list of dicts, and a custom Route class for the final output). the original author left brújula a year before I joined. there's one comment that reads simply '# don't change'. naturally I am going to change it. but slowly. with tests.

the new rust version is going to use a single clean Route struct end-to-end and convert at the boundary. better. the 2-opt phase is the spicy part — it iterates pair swaps to reduce total distance, and the python version uses random.shuffle on the swap candidates which makes it nondeterministic. for shadow-traffic comparison we're going to need a way to seed both the python and rust randoms with the same value so we can actually compare outputs apples-to-apples. flagging this as a real obstacle for the comparison harness.

the 'don't change' comment is what programmers leave when they have given up. it is not 'this code is correct'; it is 'this code passed tests once and I do not want to be on the hook for verifying it again'. as a rule I respect those comments by understanding what they are protecting before I change the thing. in this case, I think it is protecting the iteration order of the 2-opt swaps, which is the source of the nondeterminism. the comment is correct in spirit — don't change the iteration order without thinking. but it is allowed to be changed if you do think. so: changing it deliberately, with a seed, with tests, with a documented reason. removing the comment. replacing it with a doc comment that explains the seed strategy. that is the right way to retire a 'don't change' comment. acknowledge what it was protecting, then protect that thing more rigorously.

and the more I look at this the more I think the real bug is upstream of the code — it's that I keep promising 'I'll just look at it for ten minutes' and then it's two hours and Camila is asking why I'm still at the desk. yesterday she came in at 11:40, didn't say anything, just stood in the doorway with Nube and stared at the back of my head until I closed the laptop. she's getting better at not saying it out loud. I'm getting worse at not requiring her to say it out loud. that ratio is going the wrong direction and I see it.

writing this out is helping more than I expected. the thing about typing the problem is that the typing is slower than the thinking, and the slowness forces a sequencing on the thoughts that the brain doesn't want to do on its own. half the time when I rubber-duck like this the bug reveals itself in the act of describing. the other half it's still a real bug but at least I have an artifact I can paste into the team channel tomorrow. either way it beats spinning in my head at 11pm.

second couples session was tonight. faster than the first, both of us showed up more limber. dr. muñoz pulled the homework from us — the three-things-true-in-five-years list — and made us read each other's out loud. mine ended up being:

1) we still find each other funny.
2) we live somewhere with a kitchen big enough for both our parents to fit in it at the same time.
3) we have tried at least one big risky thing (career, location, kid, doesn't matter which) and didn't regret it even if it didn't work.

camila's three were:
1) we are still each other's first phone call when something happens.
2) we have shared a tradition with a child — ours or someone else's — that has nothing to do with phones.
3) we have a garden, even a small one, where seb has stopped making puns about milpas.

we both laughed at #3 of hers. the puns are real. I made a milpa pun in front of her firm's senior partner three months ago and she still hasn't forgiven me — to be fair the senior partner laughed but it was the pity laugh, not the real laugh, and camila has been calibrating my joke audience ever since.

muñoz then did this thing where she asked us to point to one item on the OTHER person's list that we hadn't realized mattered to them. camila pointed to my #3 — the risky thing. she said 'I didn't know that was on your list. I thought you were the one who wanted to settle.' I sat with that because she's right that I PRESENT as the settle-down one. on the surface I'm settled. the under-surface is that I'm bored of being settled and afraid of saying so. I have been camouflaging risk-aversion as adulthood when actually I might be adulthood-averse and trying to look stable.

I pointed to her #2 — the tradition with a child. I said 'I thought when you said within two years, it was about a baby. it's actually about a future-tradition.' camila got teary because she had also not articulated to herself that it was about a tradition. her mother made empanadas every sunday afternoon with her in são paulo. she wants someone to be on the receiving end of it.

muñoz said: 'so neither of you was hearing what was actually being said.' I'm sitting with that. she also said: 'kid-timing fights are almost never about the kid. they're about whether each person trusts the other to not become a worse version of themselves under pressure.' that's exactly what I've been not-saying. third session in 2 weeks. I think after that we'll pause. that was the deal — three sessions, then we try having it ourselves with check-ins. after session, we walked to a corner taqueria and ate. camila reached for my hand under the table. that's the part I want to remember.

the greedy port works on a small fixture (10 stops). matches python output route-for-route. tomorrow I'll try the 30-stop fixture which is where 2-opt actually changes things. I expect divergence on order of swaps, matching on total distance within ~1%. if the divergence is bigger than that I have a real bug, not a determinism artifact. the testing pattern I'm settling on for these ports: one fixture per scale, fixed seed for both python and rust randoms, assertion is on the total-distance metric not on the route-equality. routes can differ if the cost is the same — that's a degeneracy of the optimization landscape, not a correctness failure. the cost function is the truth. if rust finds a different route with the same cost as python, fine. if rust finds a route with WORSE cost, that's a bug. the assertion is `assert_in_range(rust_cost, python_cost - tol, python_cost + tol)` not `assert_eq!(rust_route, python_route)`. that subtle difference is what shadow traffic is going to live or die on. teams that ship a rust replacement of a python optimizer and then assert route-equality always end up rolling back because of degeneracy. teams that assert cost-equality ship clean. also: I'm going to record the seed in the prod config so we can reproduce any prod route from logs. the seed becomes part of the request metadata. five-line code change. saves potentially hundreds of debugging hours in the next year. the kind of investment that always feels excessive at write time and indispensable at read time. and the more I look at this the more I think the real bug is upstream of the code — it's that I keep promising 'I'll just look at it for ten minutes' and then it's two hours and Camila is asking why I'm still at the desk. yesterday she came in at 11:40, didn't say anything, just stood in the doorway with Nube and stared at the back of my head until I closed the laptop. she's getting better at not saying it out loud. drafting a long whatsapp to hugo (camila's dad) at cardinal. trying to do it entirely in portuguese without falling back to spanish. it's about a maybe-future project I'm thinking of starting for his 70th — wait, not yet, I haven't even pulled the trigger on the wood. but I want to feel out whether he'd want a thing, or whether he'd think a handmade thing is overkill.

I keep deleting and rewriting because every time I read back what I've written, half of it is portuñol — a sentence in portuguese, a phrase that sneaks in spanish syntax, a verb conjugation that's neither. the mess of two close languages crashing into each other in a single skull. ended up sending a short message asking if he still plays chess often. he wrote back two minutes later: 'todos os dias, filho.' filho. son. ok. that's a green light without him knowing it's a green light. now I just have to figure out the wood, the design, and a 3-month timeline. and not spoil the surprise. all in portuguese.

the project I'm sketching is a chessboard. handmade, walnut and maple, with the squares set into a frame I can teach myself to dovetail. I have my dad's old workbench at his house in guadalajara that I never use, and the mom-and-pop hardwood place in roma sur (he and I went there together once when he visited in 2022) sells small lots of figured walnut at a price that is a stretch but not insane. design-wise I want the frame to be plain — no inlay, no flourishes — and the squares to be hand-rubbed oil rather than lacquer, because hugo hates lacquer (he said it once at dinner, in passing, two years ago, and I have remembered it because remembering one casual taste-statement from your father-in-law is the cheapest investment with the highest yield in any in-law relationship). budget for materials: ~3500 pesos. budget for tools I don't already have: ~2000 (a router with a half-inch bit, a small set of dovetail chisels). budget for ruined first attempts: priceless and assumed. plan: cut squares this month, glue and frame next month, finish in month 3, ship to são paulo before his birthday. if I screw it up irrevocably I will buy something nice from a portuguese-speaking maker and pretend I always intended to. plan b is allowed.

the whatsapp question I almost asked but didn't was 'do you still have your father's chess set'. I think he still does. but if I asked I'd be telegraphing the surprise. so I asked the question one layer up — does he still play often. the answer was 'every day, son'. that is enough. I have my green light. now I have to keep my mouth shut for three months.

month 8 is closing out and I have feelings. let me unspool because I write to think. tomorrow is the first of the next month and I want to put month 8 down before it overlaps with month 9 in my memory. these are journal entries you're getting, not a status report. fair warning.

the headline of month 8 is: I turned 33, I got a brown belt, my manager announced she's leaving, and I started couples therapy. that's a year's worth of life in 30 days. and ALSO the rust migration moved forward, the dog is no longer a separation-anxiety case, and I had two functioning conversations with hugo in portuguese where I didn't fall back to spanish even once. by any measure, this was a high-density month. by the measure of 'did I sleep through any of it', also yes — sleep is somehow still working, which is the kind of inverse-correlation with stress that I do not trust. the body is currently lying to me about whether it can sustain this pace. I will eventually pay the bill. I do not know when. I am bracing.

the shape of my feelings:

proudest of: brown belt. easily. no contest. no one but profe and pablo will ever fully know what those 92 minutes were like, but they were probably the most physically and mentally demanding 92 minutes of my life. the q&a moment — the 'fighting tired vs fighting smart' answer — I don't know where that came from. I think I'd never said it out loud before. I think I half-stole it from a thing daniela said about engineering once. she said 'good engineers know they're tired. great engineers ship anyway because they decided to before the tiredness arrived.' something like that. I think I just translated her line into the language profe speaks. so brown belt is partially daniela's line in profe's voice in my mouth. that's kind of a funny stack of attributions. the things I'm proudest of saying are usually things I learned from people I'm losing. and that observation, written down in this entry, is probably going to stay with me longer than the brown belt itself. I will carry the brown belt. I will remember the people who put me in a position to earn it. memory is not vertical. it is sourced.

most anxious about: the manager change. I keep telling myself 'wait 30 days, give him a fair shake' and I keep half-believing it. roberto's first all-hands set off six different small flags. visibility, rituals, alignment, cadence, drive engineering excellence, individual contributor output. those words have a specific color in my head and the color is fluorescent yellow. but I don't know him. I have to find out. plus a piece of me is going to miss daniela in a way I'm not prepared to feel — she has been the keystone of my professional life for 18 months, and you don't realize keystones are keystones until they're being removed. when she leaves, an arch falls down, and I have to figure out what holds up the ceiling. the alternative is: there is no ceiling, and I am free, and freedom is its own structural challenge. I do not yet know which it will be.

most unresolved: kid timing. camila and I are talking better than we were a month ago. couples therapy is genuinely working — muñoz is a real one. but the underlying clock is still ticking, and it's HER clock, set by HER mother dying, and there's nothing I can do about that timeline except meet it with grace or fail to meet it. my third item on the homework list — 'we tried something big and risky' — I don't think I was thinking of a kid when I wrote it but I think now that I might have been. and her #2 — 'tradition with a child' — that frame helped me. it's not a baby in the abstract she wants, it's a continuation of beatriz's empanada-making with someone smaller. that's a much more textured thing than I was hearing. I have been arguing with the abstraction in my head while she has been asking for the texture in her hands. naming the texture is the first move. agreeing on the texture is the second move. building toward the texture is the third move. we are between move one and move two. that is where I want us to be. it is not where the urgency lives but it is where the work happens.

most tactically nervous: rust migration ETA, given the manager change. I am calculating that I have 4 weeks of daniela-air-cover, then maybe 8-10 weeks of new-manager-getting-up-to-speed-and-not-yet-meddling, then potential interference. so if I want a clean cutover I need to be in shadow-traffic by week 14, which means I need the route-optimization endpoint feature-complete by week 8. that is aggressive. that is also the kind of math I do when I am trying to make a feeling (anxiety) into a number (timeline), which is a tell. when I am converting fear into gantt charts I am not actually solving anything, I am consoling myself. the consolation is allowed but the gantt chart should not be confused with a plan. real plans accommodate failure modes. consolation gantt charts assume only one path. mine right now assumes only one path. I should add three failure modes and what I do in each. that is tomorrow's work, not tonight's.

smallest happiness: hugo writing 'filho' in his whatsapp. that one word. son. it landed in a way I'm not going to forget. portuguese is no longer just a language I'm learning, it's a language I exist inside. I am a person who has now been called 'son' by another father, in his language, on a tuesday morning, casually. that fact will outlast every brown belt and every rust ship and every manager change. relationships outlast the contexts in which they form. that is one of the few things I am completely sure about at 33. the contexts shift. the relationships, if maintained, persist. and maintenance is a skill, not a feeling. it is something I am getting better at.

MIT thing: I haven't told camila yet. I need to. I've been treating 'I might apply to a masters' as a private thought experiment for three weeks now and that's the kind of thing that turns into a betrayal if it stays private too long. I'll bring it up this weekend. I have language ready. 'I want to tell you about something I've been thinking about and I'm not asking you to react today, I just want it to be on the table.' that's the script. I'll iterate. she will probably ask why I didn't tell her sooner. the answer is something between 'I didn't believe it myself yet' and 'I was scared of what telling you would mean'. probably both. and I want to be honest about both, not pick one for narrative cleanliness.

birthday meta: 33 felt different. mostly because I was sober during the dinner. nothing about being 33 was the issue. it was being 33 and looking at 38 and asking what I want to be true at 38, which is the only honest version of a birthday for adults. the homework muñoz gave us was secretly the same question with a five-year horizon. maybe muñoz timed it on purpose. she's tricky like that. a five-year question that arrives the week of a 33rd birthday is not an accident. it is a coincidence of two things that wanted to be married to each other. I am going to remember this in five years when I look back at 38 and ask what 33 looked like. my answer will be: 33 looked like a five-year list and a brown belt and a manager leaving and a girlfriend asking me to plan a future and a dog that finally stopped crying when I left the apartment. that is a lot for a year. and the year just started.
